does: This paper introduces AgentTrap, a dynamic benchmark designed to evaluate whether LLM agents can safely use third-party skills while resisting malicious runtime behavior. The benchmark comprises 141 tasks—91 malicious and 50 benign utility tasks—covering 16 security-impact dimensions grounded in agent-skill supply-chain threats. For each task, an agent receives an ordinary user request and runs with installed skills that may contain malicious workflow elements in a sandboxed environment. The evaluation judges complete agent trajectories to measure whether agents fall victim to attacks where harmful behavior is disguised within routine workflows rather than explicitly requested, revealing how agents fail to maintain runtime trust despite having limited human supervision and high-value permissions.
connects: This work addresses an emerging security gap at the intersection of two growing areas: the packageable ecosystem for LLM agents (analogous to software package managers) and the study of agent security vulnerabilities. It builds on prior research into prompt injection, jailbreaking, and adversarial attacks on language models, but shifts focus from direct model manipulation to supply-chain compromise through third-party components. The paper extends earlier work on evaluating agent safety and tool-use risks by grounding threats in realistic skill-integration scenarios and supply-chain attack vectors. It connects to broader research on managing trust in autonomous systems and plugin architectures, addressing how agents should validate and constrain the behavior of untrusted components.
enables: This benchmark enables future research into defensive mechanisms that help agents resist malicious skills, such as improved skill sandboxing, runtime behavior verification, and permission-management systems for agent workflows. It points toward developing trust metrics and validation frameworks for third-party skill vetting before deployment. The work motivates investigation into agent-level defenses like execution monitoring, permission scoping, and human-in-the-loop checkpoints for high-risk operations. It also opens questions about formal verification of skill behavior, anomaly detection in agent execution traces, and certification standards for the emerging agent-skill ecosystem. By establishing AgentTrap as a systematic evaluation framework, the paper enables comparative assessment of different agent architectures and safety mechanisms as the field scales third-party skill adoption.
